message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>As the SARS-CoV-2 virus mutates, mutations harboured in patients become increasingly diverse. Patients classified into two strains may have overlapping non-variant-defining mutations.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  Mutation calling by sequencing is relative to a
                  <jats:italic>reference genome</jats:italic>
                  . As SARS-CoV-2 mutates, tracking emerging mutant strains may become increasingly problematic if the reference genome remains Wuhan-Hu-1, because the comparison then becomes
                  <jats:italic>indirect</jats:italic>
                  : current dominant strain relative to Wuhan-Hu-1 versus emerging strain relative to Wuhan-Hu-1.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The original Thermo Fisher’s TaqPath PCR test, on which the UK has standardized national testing of SARS-CoV-2 primarily, targets Wuhan-Hu-1. PCR targets appear readily updated, as TaqPath 2.0 now targets both currently known and future SARS-CoV-2 mutations, probing the N gene and ORF1ab but not the S gene, with 8 probes instead of the original 3 probes. Going forward, our statistical method can more
                  <jats:italic>directly</jats:italic>
                  compare current wildtype versus emerging mutants, since our new method can use any pair of probes updated to probe the current wildtype and anticipated mutations.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The fact that patients harbour mixtures of mutations allows our statistical methods to potentially catch emerging mutants. Given a PCR test which targets the current dominant strain (current wildtype), our statistical method can potentially
                  <jats:italic>directly</jats:italic>
                  differentiate the current wildtype from an emerging strain.
                </jats:p>
title: Statistical Design and Analysis of Diagnostic Tests for Mutating Viruses
